content.text: Engineering teams design retention windows primarily for operational reasons — keep hot logs for 30 days because that covers most incident debugging, age them to warm storage for 90 days for trend analysis, then delete. Compliance requirements impose a separate, non-negotiable floor: regulators and auditors specify minimum periods during which evidence must remain available and unaltered. For a Claude application, the most relevant frameworks are SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, HIPAA (if you handle health data), PCI-DSS (if your product touches payments), and ISO 27001.

The practical rule is simple: retain each data category for the longest required period across all applicable frameworks. This document maps each telemetry type to its compliance floor, explains the legal mechanics of legal holds and erasure, and shows how to encode these requirements as infrastructure-as-code that CI enforces.

attrs.alt: Compliance-driven retention matrix: SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001 requirements per data type

content.text: SOC 2 does not mandate specific retention periods by number, but auditors expect that evidence covering the entire audit period (typically 12 months) is available. Access logs, change management records, and incident evidence must all span the audit window. In practice: keep audit and access logs for at least 1 year, and keep them in an unaltered state — a log store where records can be overwritten or deleted will fail a SOC 2 controls test.
GDPR imposes two obligations that directly affect log retention:
(Article 5(1)(c) — collect only what you need) and

(Article 5(1)(e) — keep data no longer than necessary). For telemetry, this means you must justify every log field against a specific operational purpose. Logs that contain personal data (user IDs, IP addresses, session tokens) fall under GDPR whether or not they are labelled as PII.

Article 17 gives data subjects the right to erasure. If a user requests deletion of their data, you must be able to locate and delete their records from every tier — including cold archives — within a documented SLA. The practical implication is that either (a) you do not log any field that uniquely identifies a person, or (b) you maintain a mapping from user ID to every log record that references them and implement a deletion pipeline across tiers.

apply PII scrubbing before any log leaves the application process. Replace user IDs with a one-way hash derived from a rotating secret so the hash cannot be reversed even if logs are leaked. This turns most GDPR erasure obligations into a key-rotation problem rather than a record-search-and-delete problem.

HIPAA applies when your Claude application processes Protected Health Information (PHI). The Security Rule (45 CFR §164.312(b)) requires audit controls — mechanisms that record and examine access to systems containing PHI. The minimum retention period for HIPAA documentation is 6 years from creation or last effective date. If your Claude application generates, processes, or transmits clinical text, you must treat it as a covered component and retain all audit and access logs for the HIPAA 6-year floor.

PCI-DSS Requirement 10 mandates that log data for cardholder-environment systems is retained for at least 12 months, with the most recent 3 months immediately available (i.e., in hot or warm tier). If Claude processes or generates text related to payment card data — even indirectly as a support chatbot — the system is in scope and must meet these log retention requirements.

The "Effective Window" column is the minimum you must retain. You may retain longer at your discretion, but holding data beyond the maximum increases your breach exposure and GDPR liability. Define both a minimum and a maximum retention window in your Data Retention Policy document.

A legal hold (also called a litigation hold) is a directive to preserve records that may be relevant to anticipated or active litigation. Legal holds override all retention schedules — data under hold must not be deleted even if it has passed its maximum retention window.

# scripts/check-deletion-safe.py
# Run before any deletion job to verify no legal hold blocks it.

import sys
from pathlib import Path
from models.legal_hold import load_holds, is_on_hold

def main(resource: str) -> int:
    if is_on_hold(resource):
        active = [h for h in load_holds() if h.is_active() and h.covers(resource)]
        for h in active:
            print(
                f"BLOCKED: '{resource}' is under legal hold '{h.hold_id}' "
                f"(case: {h.case_reference}, custodian: {h.custodian})",
                file=sys.stderr,
            )
        return 1

    print(f"OK: '{resource}' is not under any active legal hold")
    return 0

if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main(sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else ""))

When a data subject submits an Article 17 erasure request, your obligation is to delete all personal data referencing that subject from every system within a reasonable time. For logs, the standard approach when PII scrubbing is already in place is to rotate the hashing secret for that user's hash key — making their previous log entries un-linkable — rather than scanning every log file.

This approach satisfies GDPR Article 17 pseudonymisation requirements — once the key is deleted the data is effectively anonymous — without requiring you to scan and physically delete records from cold Glacier archives during the 30-day SLA window.

If logs contain fields that cannot be pseudonymised (e.g., verbatim error messages that echoed user input before PII scrubbing was deployed), physical deletion is necessary. Document the SLA per tier:
Encoding Compliance Requirements as Infrastructure Code

Compliance requirements should be written into your IaC, not documented in a spreadsheet that drifts from reality. The following Terraform snippet applies the correct S3 Object Lock configuration and lifecycle rules for audit logs, derived from the 7-year compliance floor.
Add a CI job that reads your IaC or live infrastructure and validates that every log store satisfies the compliance floor for its data classification.
Regulators want evidence of a documented policy, not just evidence that you happened to retain the right data. Maintain two documents:

a human-readable document mapping each data category to its retention window (minimum and maximum), the applicable framework requirement, the storage location, and the deletion method. Update it when you change IaC.

if your Claude application processes personal data on behalf of customers, the DPA must state the retention periods and deletion procedures you commit to. Your DPA feeds your customers' own GDPR compliance obligations.

Both documents should be version-controlled alongside the IaC that implements them. A drift between the policy document and the live infrastructure is itself a compliance finding — treat the policy doc as spec and IaC validation as the test.

Compliance requirements impose a non-negotiable floor on how long you must keep telemetry data. For most Claude applications, the binding constraints are SOC 2 (1 year for audit/access logs) and GDPR (data minimisation + erasure SLA). HIPAA extends the floor to 6 years if health data is involved.
Retain each data type for the longest required period across all applicable frameworks.
Audit and access logs need 7 years (HIPAA/GDPR) — use immutable object storage.
Apply PII scrubbing before logging to convert GDPR erasure from a scan-and-delete problem to a key-rotation problem.
Maintain a legal hold register and check it before any deletion job runs.
Encode retention requirements in Terraform/IaC — a policy doc without enforcing code will drift.
Run compliance validation in CI daily to catch buckets created without lifecycle rules.
